title: Standing Up a Governed Landing Zone Fast
description:
## A Multi-Account Foundation, Quickly

A company with no existing multi-account structure wants to establish a **governed multi-account environment** in a matter of days, with:

- a pre-built OU structure plus dedicated **log archive** and **audit/security** accounts,
- **baseline guardrails** (preventive and detective) applied automatically,
- a repeatable, self-service way for teams to **provision new accounts** that are compliant from day one,
- minimal custom engineering and an ongoing dashboard of compliance status.

Which approach best meets these goals?

options.text: Use **AWS Control Tower** to deploy a landing zone with its baseline guardrails, log archive and audit accounts, and **Account Factory** for governed account provisioning.
Build everything by hand with **AWS Organizations**, hand-written SCPs, and **CloudFormation StackSets**, wiring up logging accounts and guardrails yourself.
Deploy **AWS Config** with conformance packs as the complete solution for the multi-account foundation.
Create the accounts manually and rely on **IAM Identity Center** to provide governance and guardrails.

explanation: **AWS Control Tower** is purpose-built to stand up a **well-architected, governed multi-account landing zone automatically**: it creates the OU structure, the dedicated **log archive** and **audit** accounts, applies a set of preventive (SCP-based) and detective (Config-based) **guardrails**, and provides **Account Factory** for self-service, compliant account provisioning — all with a dashboard, minimal custom work, and in a short time. Option B (Organizations + SCPs + StackSets) *can* produce a similar result but requires you to design and build every piece yourself — far slower and more error-prone, which is exactly what Control Tower automates on top of these same primitives. Option C (**AWS Config**) only provides resource configuration tracking and compliance detection; it is a *component* of governance, not a landing-zone builder, and offers no account factory or OU/SCP setup. Option D (**IAM Identity Center**) handles workforce sign-on, not guardrails or account provisioning.

hints.title: Automated vs. do-it-yourself
hints.body: Organizations + StackSets can reach the same end state, but Control Tower automates the landing zone so you don't hand-build it.

hints.title: Listen for 'Account Factory' cues
hints.body: Self-service provisioning of compliant accounts, baseline guardrails, and log/audit accounts out of the box = Control Tower.
